patterns.name: Repository
patterns.intent: Encapsulate data access logic behind a collection-like interface, decoupling the domain layer from persistence concerns.
patterns.problem: Business logic becomes tangled with database queries, file I/O, or API calls. Changing the storage backend requires modifying domain code. Testing business logic requires a real database.
patterns.solution: Define an abstract interface that looks like a collection (get, list, filter, add, remove). Implement it concretely for each storage backend. The domain layer depends only on the abstract interface.
when_to_use: When you need to swap storage backends (JSON to SQLite, SQLite to Postgres) without touching business logic
When you want to test business logic without a real database using a mock or in-memory repository
When multiple parts of the application need to access the same data with a consistent interface
When data access logic is complex enough to warrant isolation
when_not_to_use: For simple scripts where a direct database call is clearer and the abstraction adds no value
When there is only ever one storage backend and no testing requirement
When the overhead of an extra layer slows delivery with no architectural benefit
advantages: Domain logic is completely decoupled from persistence technology
Storage backend can be swapped by changing one class
Business logic tests run without a database using a mock repository
Data access logic is centralised and not scattered across services
disadvantages: Adds an extra layer of abstraction that must be maintained
Can be over-engineering for simple CRUD applications
Leaky abstractions can emerge if the interface exposes database-specific concepts
examples: Jarvis AcademyRepository ABC with JsonAcademyRepository as the concrete implementation
Jarvis KnowledgeRepository ABC with JsonKnowledgeRepository as the concrete implementation
A UserRepository interface with SqlUserRepository and InMemoryUserRepository implementations
references: Martin Fowler — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Chapter 10)

patterns.name: Factory
patterns.intent: Centralise object creation logic so callers are decoupled from the concrete types they create.
patterns.problem: When a caller constructs objects directly with 'new' or direct instantiation, it becomes coupled to the concrete type. Adding a new variant requires modifying every call site. Construction logic that belongs together gets duplicated across the codebase.
patterns.solution: Define a factory function or class whose single responsibility is creating and returning objects. The caller asks the factory for an object; it never constructs one directly. Factories can return an abstract type, hiding which concrete class was instantiated.
when_to_use: When the exact type of object to create is determined at runtime (e.g. based on configuration or user input)
When construction logic is complex and should not be duplicated at call sites
When you want callers to depend on an abstraction rather than a concrete class
When a family of related objects must be created consistently
when_not_to_use: When there is only one concrete type and no variation is expected
When the construction is trivial enough that a factory adds complexity without benefit
When direct instantiation is clearer and the indirection makes the code harder to follow
advantages: Callers are decoupled from concrete types
New variants can be added by extending the factory without modifying callers
Complex construction logic is centralised in one place
Makes switching implementations easier
disadvantages: Adds indirection that can make code harder to trace
Can become a dumping ground for unrelated construction logic
Overuse creates unnecessary complexity in simple systems
examples: A RepositoryFactory that reads configuration and returns either a JsonRepository or SqlRepository
A WorkerFactory in Jarvis Genesis-020 that creates the appropriate Engineering Worker type based on task category
Python's logging.getLogger() — callers never instantiate Logger directly
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Factory Method, Abstract Factory)

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.intent: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable at runtime without modifying the context that uses them.
patterns.problem: A class needs to perform an operation, but the exact algorithm varies. Hard-coding if/elif chains for each variant makes the class grow every time a new algorithm is needed, violating the Open/Closed Principle.
patterns.solution: Extract each algorithm into its own class behind a shared interface. The context class receives the chosen strategy via constructor injection or a setter, and delegates the operation to it. Strategies are interchangeable because they share the same interface.
when_to_use: When multiple variants of an algorithm exist and must be selectable at runtime
When you want to eliminate conditional logic that selects between algorithm variants
When algorithms should be independently testable without loading the full context
When new algorithms may be added in the future without modifying existing code
when_not_to_use: When there is only one algorithm and no variation is expected
When the number of strategies is small and stable, and a simple conditional is clearer
When clients must be aware of strategy differences to use the system correctly
advantages: Algorithms are independently testable and swappable
Adding a new strategy requires only a new class — no modification to the context
Eliminates complex conditional logic from the context
Follows Open/Closed and Dependency Inversion Principles
disadvantages: Increases the number of classes in the system
Clients must know which strategy to choose, which can leak implementation knowledge
Overhead of the abstraction is not justified when only one algorithm exists
examples: A SearchStrategy interface with KeywordSearchStrategy and SubstringSearchStrategy implementations — the AcademyService could accept a search strategy
A SortStrategy interface with AlphabeticalSort and PrioritySort for ordering Engineering Worker queues
Python's sort() accepting a key function — the key function is the strategy
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Strategy)
Robert C. Martin — Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices

patterns.name: Adapter
patterns.intent: Convert the interface of an existing class into the interface a client expects, allowing incompatible interfaces to work together without modifying either.
patterns.problem: You have an existing component with a useful implementation, but its interface does not match what your system expects. You cannot modify the existing component (it is a third-party library, a frozen module, or a legacy system).
patterns.solution: Create an Adapter class that implements the interface your system expects, and internally delegates calls to the existing component, translating between the two interfaces.
when_to_use: When integrating a third-party library whose interface does not match your system's abstractions
When reusing a legacy component without modifying it
When two independently developed modules need to interoperate
When writing an abstraction layer over an external API so it can be swapped later
when_not_to_use: When you can modify the original class directly — adapting is a workaround, not a first choice
When the impedance mismatch is so large that the adapter becomes more complex than a rewrite
When the adaptation is trivial enough that it does not warrant a full class
advantages: Integrates existing components without modifying their source
Isolates integration complexity inside the adapter, keeping the rest of the system clean
Enables swapping external dependencies by changing only the adapter
disadvantages: Adds an extra class and indirection for each adapted component
Can hide impedance mismatch rather than solving it properly
A poorly designed adapter leaks the adapted interface back to the caller
examples: A JarvisOpenAIAdapter that wraps the OpenAI SDK and implements a Jarvis LanguageModelProvider interface
A TavilySearchAdapter that wraps the Tavily API and implements a Jarvis WebSearchProvider interface
Python's io.TextIOWrapper adapting a binary stream to a text stream interface
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Adapter)
Martin Fowler — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture

patterns.name: Facade
patterns.intent: Provide a simplified, unified interface to a complex subsystem, reducing the learning curve and coupling for clients.
patterns.problem: A subsystem is internally complex, composed of many classes with interdependencies. Clients that use the subsystem directly must understand all of its internals, creating tight coupling and making the subsystem hard to change.
patterns.solution: Create a Facade class that exposes a simple, high-level interface covering the most common use cases of the subsystem. The facade delegates to the subsystem's classes internally. Clients interact with the facade, not the internals.
when_to_use: When a subsystem is complex and clients only need a subset of its functionality
When you want to layer a system so higher layers have a clean entry point into lower layers
When you are wrapping a legacy system to present a modern interface
When you want to reduce the coupling surface between a client and a complex subsystem
when_not_to_use: When clients genuinely need fine-grained access to the subsystem internals
When the subsystem is simple enough that a facade adds no value
When the facade becomes a God Object, absorbing too much responsibility
advantages: Dramatically simplifies the interface for common use cases
Decouples clients from subsystem internals — the subsystem can evolve without affecting clients
Creates a natural layering boundary in the architecture
disadvantages: Can become a God Object if responsibilities are not carefully bounded
Clients that need full subsystem power are not served by the facade
A facade that is too thin adds indirection without value
examples: AcademyService is a facade over AcademyRepository and AcademyLoader — callers interact with the service without knowing how data is loaded or stored
The Jarvis EngineeringCoordinator (Genesis-018) is a facade over the queue, dispatcher, and worker registry
A JarvisOS class that exposes start(), stop(), run_task() without exposing internal subsystem wiring
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Facade)

patterns.name: Builder
patterns.intent: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing the same construction process to produce different representations.
patterns.problem: A class requires many parameters or a multi-step construction process. Telescoping constructors (many optional parameters) are unreadable. Setting fields after construction leaves objects in invalid intermediate states.
patterns.solution: Introduce a Builder class with methods for setting each part of the object. Each method returns the builder itself to allow chaining. A final build() method validates and returns the fully constructed, immutable object.
when_to_use: When constructing an object requires many parameters, especially optional ones
When the object must be immutable once created but construction requires multiple steps
When the same construction steps should produce different representations
When construction requires validation that should happen before the object is returned
when_not_to_use: When the object has only one or two required fields — a constructor is simpler
When the object is mutable and can be configured after construction
When the additional class complexity is not justified by the construction problem
advantages: Eliminates telescoping constructors with many optional parameters
Objects are only returned in a valid, complete state
Construction steps are readable and self-documenting
The same builder process can produce different object types
disadvantages: Requires an extra Builder class for each target class
Overkill for objects with simple construction requirements
Mutable builder state before build() is called can be a source of bugs if the builder is shared
examples: A DiagnosticReportBuilder in Jarvis Genesis-017 that accumulates evidence, failures, and recommendations before producing an immutable report
Python's subprocess.Popen built via a command list and keyword arguments
An EngineeeringTaskBuilder that sets task type, priority, target file, and constraints before producing a frozen EngineeringTask
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Builder)

patterns.name: Observer
patterns.intent: Define a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified and updated automatically.
patterns.problem: When one object changes state, an unknown number of other objects need to react. Hard-coding the notification calls couples the subject to every observer, making the system rigid and violating the Open/Closed Principle.
patterns.solution: Define a Subject that maintains a list of Observer objects and notifies them by calling a standard update() method when its state changes. Observers register and deregister themselves. The Subject knows nothing about the concrete Observer types.
when_to_use: When a change in one object requires updating others, and you do not know how many objects will need to change
When you want to decouple the object that triggers an event from the objects that respond to it
When objects should be able to register interest in events at runtime
When building event-driven or reactive systems
when_not_to_use: When the set of observers is fixed and known at compile time — direct calls are clearer
When notification order matters and the Observer pattern does not guarantee it
When cascading updates (observer triggers another observer) can create difficult-to-debug cycles
advantages: Subject and observers are loosely coupled — the subject does not know concrete observer types
New observers can be added without modifying the subject
Supports broadcast communication to multiple interested parties
disadvantages: Unexpected updates and cascading notifications can be hard to trace and debug
Observers that forget to deregister cause memory leaks
No guarantee on notification order, which can matter in some systems
examples: A Jarvis SessionEventBus where Engineering Workers subscribe to session state changes
A HealthMonitor that notifies registered listeners when a subsystem check fails
Python's logging system — handlers (observers) subscribe to loggers (subjects)
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Observer)
Martin Fowler — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Event-based patterns)

patterns.name: Command
patterns.intent: Encapsulate a request as an object, allowing it to be queued, logged, undone, or parameterised without the caller knowing the receiver's implementation.
patterns.problem: A caller needs to trigger an operation on a receiver, but the caller should not be coupled to how the receiver implements the operation. Operations may need to be queued, retried, logged, or undone.
patterns.solution: Define a Command interface with an execute() method. Each concrete Command encapsulates a receiver and the action to perform on it. The invoker holds and executes commands without knowing what they do internally.
when_to_use: When you need to queue or schedule operations for later execution
When operations need to be logged so they can be audited or replayed
When undo/redo functionality is required
When the caller should be decoupled from the receiver and the operation details
When building a task queue or workflow engine
when_not_to_use: When the operation is simple and immediate, and the indirection adds no value
When undo, queuing, and logging are never needed
When the number of command types would explode the class count beyond maintainability
advantages: Decouples the object that invokes the operation from the one that knows how to perform it
Commands can be queued, scheduled, logged, and replayed
Undo support is natural — each command can hold the state needed to reverse itself
New commands can be added without changing the invoker
disadvantages: Results in a large number of small command classes
Simple operations are made verbose by wrapping them in a class
Undo logic can become complex and error-prone
examples: Jarvis Engineering Queue (Genesis-018) dispatches EngineeringTask objects that are a form of command
A RepairCommand encapsulating a specific code fix that the Debugging subsystem can execute, log, and roll back
Python's asyncio task scheduling — coroutines submitted to an event loop are command-like
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Command)
Robert C. Martin — Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices

patterns.name: Dependency Injection
patterns.intent: Supply a component's dependencies from outside rather than letting the component construct them internally, enabling flexibility, testability, and adherence to the Dependency Inversion Principle.
patterns.problem: When a class constructs its own dependencies internally, it is tightly coupled to specific implementations. Changing an implementation requires modifying the class. Testing the class requires the real dependency to be present and functional.
patterns.solution: Pass dependencies into the component via its constructor, a method, or a property. The component declares what it needs (the abstraction) but does not decide how to obtain it. The calling code or a DI container is responsible for constructing and injecting the correct implementation.
when_to_use: When a class has external dependencies (databases, APIs, file systems, services) that should be swappable
When you want to test a class in isolation by injecting a mock or stub dependency
When the same class should work with different dependency implementations in different environments
As the default approach for wiring together any non-trivial system
when_not_to_use: For truly stateless utility functions with no external dependencies
When a DI container introduces more complexity than the problem it solves
When the dependency is a stable, implementation-level detail that genuinely should not vary
advantages: Classes are decoupled from specific dependency implementations
Dependencies can be replaced with mocks for isolated unit testing
The same class works in different environments by injecting different implementations
Wiring is explicit and visible at the composition root, not hidden inside classes
disadvantages: Wiring dependencies manually becomes verbose in large systems
DI containers add indirection that can make startup code hard to trace
Overuse can make code feel bureaucratic with many thin classes existing only to be injected
examples: AcademyService(repository: AcademyRepository) — the repository is injected, not constructed internally
JsonAcademyRepository(principles_path, loader=None) — the loader is injectable for testing
All Jarvis subsystems receive their dependencies via constructor injection
references: Martin Fowler — Inversion of Control Containers and the Dependency Injection pattern (martinfowler.com, 2004)
Mark Seemann — Dependency Injection Principles, Practices, and Patterns
